title: Mississippi pot roast
subtitle: Slow-cooked beef chuck with ranch seasoning, pepperoncini, and butter
excerpt: A legendary slow-cooker recipe: beef chuck, a packet of ranch seasoning, a packet of au jus mix, a stick of butter, and a jar of pepperoncini, cooked together for 8 hours until the beef is impossibly tender and the liquid has reduced to a rich, tangy, buttery gravy. Served over mashed potato or with crusty bread.
sourceNotes: Mississippi pot roast was developed in the early 2000s by Robin Chapman of Columbus, Mississippi. The recipe was shared widely through church cookbooks and regional food publications before going viral on recipe-sharing websites in the 2010s, becoming one of the most widely shared slow-cooker recipes in American home cooking. This version adapts the original for ingredients widely available in the UK, making the seasoning from scratch.

content.text: The original recipe uses two American convenience packets (ranch dressing mix and gravy mix), and the version here makes both from scratch with dried herbs and spices. The flavour is the same: savoury, slightly tangy, deeply beefy. The pepperoncini add acid and heat that cut through the rich butter. The dish has almost no water added. The beef, butter, and peppers make their own liquid as they cook.

This is one of the easiest things you can cook and one of the most rewarding. Eight hours at 150°C in a covered casserole, or 8 hours in a slow cooker on low, and the beef falls into tender shreds at the touch of a fork. The gravy needs nothing more than skimming the fat from the surface and serving as it is.

items.name: Pepperoncini
items.prepNote: whole, from a jar, plus 3 tbsp of the brine

content.text: Heat the oven to 150°C fan. Mix together the ranch seasoning ingredients. Pat the {{beef-chuck}} of beef chuck dry and sear in the casserole over high heat for 3 to 4 minutes per side until browned all over. Remove from the heat. Scatter the ranch seasoning over the beef and turn to coat. Scatter the {{pepperoncini}} of pepperoncini around the beef and pour over the brine, {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, and {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce. Place the {{unsalted-butter}} pieces on top of the beef. Cover tightly and cook for 8 hours. The beef is ready when it shreds easily with two forks.

Lift the beef onto a board and shred into large pieces with two forks. Skim the excess fat from the surface of the braising liquid. Return the shredded beef to the liquid, stir to coat, and rest for 10 minutes. Serve over mashed potato or with crusty bread, with the juices spooned over.

items.fix: Return for a further hour, the transition from tough to tender can feel sudden. The meat is done when two forks pull it apart without resistance
items.cause: Chuck varies; some pieces need longer
items.symptom: Beef is still tough after 8 hours

items.fix: Reduce the brine to 2 tbsp and taste the braising liquid before serving; add a splash of water to dilute if needed
Skim the liquid with a spoon after cooking. Or chill it overnight. The fat sets on the surface and lifts off easily.
items.cause: Beef chuck has a high fat content that renders during the long cook
items.symptom: Too much fat in the braising liquid

items.fix: Use fewer pepperoncini and cut back on the brine. The peppers give background tang, not a dominant heat.
items.cause: Too much brine or the peppers were particularly sharp

content.text: Mississippi pot roast is one of the few internet-born recipes. Robin Chapman of Columbus, Mississippi made the first version in the early 2000s. It spread by word of mouth and church cookbooks for a decade before going viral on recipe sites around 2013. Within a few years it had been cooked by millions of American home cooks and written up in every major food magazine. The appeal is plain: five ingredients, almost no prep, and a result that punches above the method. It is a modern American comfort food with an origin story you can trace to one person.
